text:
Team GB star Henry Fieldman has become the first person to ever win anOlympicmedal in both men and women’s events. Fieldman claimed his second bronze medal at the Paris 2024 Games on Saturday as Team GB came third in the women’s eight rowing.

He etches his name into the history books, having already won bronze in the men’s eight at Tokyo 2020. The 36-year-old was able to achieve the extraordinary feat thanks to his unique role as a coxswain in rowing.

Coxswains are allowed to be included in a team with rowers of the opposite sex following a rule change in 2017. Their primary role is to be in charge of guiding the boat - sitting at the stern, steering, calling the stroke and generally implementing the strategy of races.



Team GB finished with a time of 5:59.51, finishing behind runners-up Canada and gold medallists Romania. Fieldman stated following the triumph that he hopes the bronze medal will lead to the women’s eight becoming regular medallists at future global events.

"I hope that now that we've had two Olympic medals in the women's eight that this could be the start of more women's eights medals to come - stepping on to greater things," he said.

The team’s rowers were: Heidi Long, Rowan McKellar, Holly Dunford, Emily Ford, Lauren Irwin, Eve Stewart, Harriet Taylor and Annie Campbell-Orde.

Elsewhere, Great Britain defied the odds by winning gold in the men’s eight. The British boat traded the lead with the Netherlands before surging ahead in the third quarter of the 2,000m race, and won by 1.08 seconds after clocking in a time of 5:22.8.

Discovery+is the new home of the Olympics. The streamer is set to broadcast 3,800 hours of sport live from Paris.

Sports fans can getDiscovery+ for £3.99a month until the end of 2024 via Amazon Prime's video channels. This also comes with anAmazon Prime 30-day free trial.



Fieldman’s success in both male and female events follows heated debate over the inclusion of transgender athletes at the Olympics. Algerian boxer Imane Khelif - who was born female and has never identified as male - has been inaccurately described as transgender by various public figures such J.K. Rowling and Donald Trump.

The controversy came when she defeated Italian boxer Angela Carini, who abandoned their bout inside 46 seconds and failed to shake the Algerian’s hand. She was heard repeatedly saying “it’s not right,” and commented afterwards: “I’ve never been hit with such a powerful punch."

We use your sign-up to provide content in ways you've consented to and to improve our understanding of you. This may include adverts from us and 3rd parties based on our understanding. You can unsubscribe at any time. Read ourPrivacy Policy

Khelif last year was disqualified from the women’s World Championships in New Delhi for failing a gender eligibility test - the contents of which remain unknown. It was not specified why the now-banned and Russia-led International Boxing Association (IBA), which carried out the supposed tests, said Khelif "failed to meet the eligibility criteria for participating in the women's competition”.

The International Olympics Committee addressed the IBA’s murky tests this week, saying: "This is not a transgender issue. These women have been competing in competitions for many years. The testosterone is not a perfect test. Many women can have elevated levels of testosterone, which is in what would be called 'male levels' and still be women and still compete as women."

title: First Olympic medallist in both men and women's events is from Team GB | Other | Sport | Express.co.uk
author: Kasra Moradi
description: Team GB rowing star Henry Fieldman became the first athlete in history to win a medal in both men and women's events after claiming bronze in Paris.
